questionText: Before marrying Dana, Lee, a member of a fraternal insurance association, orally told Dana that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate. Dana married Lee. No written agreement memorializing that promise was ever signed by Lee. After the marriage, Lee filed the association’s standard form naming Dana as beneficiary. The association’s by-laws allowed a member to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and several years afterward Lee followed that procedure and named his two children from an earlier marriage as the new beneficiaries. When Lee died, Dana claimed the proceeds based on Lee’s pre-marriage promise and his earlier designation of her after the wedding. The children claimed the proceeds under the later beneficiary change. Who has the better claim to the insurance proceeds? Analyze.

responses.responseText:
Dana’s strongest argument is that Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise induced her to marry him and that, at least initially, Lee acted consistently with the promise by filing the association’s standard form naming Dana as beneficiary right after the wedding. However, on the facts given, the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

1) The fraternal association’s bylaws control who is entitled to the benefit, and the member complied with them
Fraternal benefit certificates are governed by the certificate and the association’s bylaws. Those bylaws here expressly permit a member to change beneficiaries later by filing the required sworn request. After several years, Lee used that permitted procedure and named his two children as new beneficiaries. When a member follows the bylaws’ beneficiary-change mechanism, the later designation controls the association’s obligation to pay.

Because the association bylaws establish a clear, contractually authorized method for changing beneficiaries, Dana cannot use an earlier informal promise to override the later, properly executed designation—unless Dana can establish an enforceable contract that made the beneficiary designation irrevocable.

2) Dana’s claim depends on enforcing Lee’s oral promise to make her an irrevocable beneficiary
Dana’s theory is essentially that Lee entered a contract with her (formed before marriage) to make her the beneficiary, presumably on a non-revocable basis. But no written agreement was signed memorializing that promise. In many jurisdictions, an agreement to make a “particular disposition” at death (functionally similar to a will-contract) is treated as within the statute-of-frauds-type requirements for enforceability, and an oral promise of this kind is generally not enforceable to prevent the promisor from changing beneficiaries later—especially where the insurance agreement/bylaws make beneficiary designations revocable until a valid, enforceable contract says otherwise.

Even putting statute of frauds aside, Dana’s oral promise is not shown to have been embodied in an enforceable “irrevocability” arrangement. Lee was permitted by the bylaws to change beneficiaries, and nothing in the facts shows a legally binding written restriction on that power.

3) Lee’s initial designation of Dana does not, by itself, defeat the later valid change
Dana points to Lee’s post-wedding act of filing the standard form naming her. That act supports that Lee initially intended to carry out the promise. But it does not establish that Lee agreed he could not later change beneficiaries. Under the bylaws, absent an enforceable restriction making the designation irrevocable, the member retains the right to revise the beneficiary designation.

4) Best overall result: the children’s later designation controls payment; Dana’s likely remedy is against Lee’s estate (if any) rather than entitlement to proceeds
Accordingly, when Lee died, the children were the beneficiaries under the association’s validly filed later change request, so they have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

Dana may have had (at most) a claim for breach of promise or related equitable relief against Lee or his estate—depending on the jurisdiction’s approach to oral promises and whether reliance (the marriage) can remove statute-of-frauds-type barriers. But based on the information given, she cannot establish a superior entitlement to the proceeds over beneficiaries designated in compliance with the association’s bylaws.

Conclusion: The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds, because Lee complied with the fraternal association’s bylaws in making a later beneficiary change, and Dana’s contrary claim rests on an unenforceable (or at least non-irrevocable) oral promise.

Dana’s claim to the proceeds based on Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise is likely enforceable only as a personal claim against Lee’s estate (or the extent of any damages for breach), not as a right to defeat the later beneficiary designation made in accordance with the association’s governing rules. The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

1) What determines entitlement to the proceeds
A fraternal insurance association’s certificate is governed by the certificate and the association’s by-laws, which are treated as part of the contract. Those by-laws here expressly allow the member to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. That means:

- The beneficiary designation is not “locked in” at the time it is first filed.
- Unless the member has bound himself to make the beneficiary designation irrevocable (typically by a valid written agreement or similar limitation recognized by the association), the member retains the contractual power to change the beneficiary until death.

Because Lee later complied with the by-laws and filed the required sworn request naming his children, the association’s contract points to the children as the proper beneficiaries at Lee’s death.

2) Effect of Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise to make Dana the beneficiary
An oral promise to designate a particular beneficiary under a life insurance certificate does not ordinarily override later, properly executed beneficiary changes.

Even if Lee’s oral promise created a moral obligation or potential breach-of-promise liability, Dana still faces two major obstacles to claiming the proceeds:

A. No valid irrevocable beneficiary arrangement
The problem for Dana is that there was “no written agreement memorializing that promise” and there is no indication that the promise was sufficient to make Dana an irrevocable beneficiary under the association’s by-laws or under generally applicable contract principles for such arrangements. Where the insurer/association retains the member’s right to change beneficiaries, courts generally treat a beneficiary as having, at most, an expectancy until death—subject to the insured’s contractual right to change.

B. Contract enforceability concerns (including typical Statute of Frauds issues)
Agreements to make another person a beneficiary under life insurance or similar instruments often fall within Statute of Frauds requirements (i.e., they must be in writing to be enforceable) or otherwise require formal proof when the promised interest is intended to survive beyond death. Dana has only an oral promise and no writing signed by Lee. That makes Dana’s effort to transform the promise into a direct claim to the proceeds much weaker.

As a result, the oral promise is more plausibly treated as giving Dana a potential claim for breach of contract against Lee’s estate (damages), rather than a claim that the insurance proceeds must be paid to her despite the insured’s later compliance with the by-laws.

3) Why the later beneficiary designation controls here
After Dana and Lee married, Lee filed the standard form naming Dana. But several years later, Lee exercised the exact procedure the by-laws permit—he filed a sworn request naming the children as new beneficiaries. Under the contract framework of fraternal benefit certificates:

- The later valid designation supersedes the earlier one.
- Dana’s status is cut off when Lee properly changes the beneficiary before death.
- Upon Lee’s death, the association must pay the beneficiaries designated at that time—here, the children.

Conclusion
The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds because Lee had the contractual right under the association’s by-laws to change beneficiaries, and he did so by filing the required sworn request naming his children. Dana’s oral pre-marriage promise, without a written agreement or other basis to make the designation irrevocable, is unlikely to defeat the later beneficiary change; it may support only a separate breach-of-promise claim against Lee’s estate, not entitlement to the proceeds.

Dana’s and the children’s competing claims turn on what rights Lee’s promise (and Dana’s initial designation) created, and whether Lee retained the contractual power—under the association’s bylaws—to change the beneficiary.

A fraternal insurance association’s bylaws are generally part of the insurance contract between the member and the association. Here, the bylaws expressly allowed the member to change beneficiaries later by filing the required sworn request. That means Dana’s designation in the standard form after the marriage ordinarily created only a revocable expectancy, not a vested right that could not be displaced by a later compliant change.

1) Effect of Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise
Dana can argue that Lee’s promise—“if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary”—created an enforceable contract, and that by marrying Lee she provided the consideration (marriage in reliance on the promise). If Lee had bound himself to make the beneficiary designation irrevocable, Dana could claim an equitable/contractual interest that would limit Lee’s ability to change beneficiaries later (and potentially support a constructive trust against the later beneficiaries).

However, in this fact pattern, the promise was oral and was never memorialized in a written agreement. In many jurisdictions, an oral promise to create a non-testamentary benefit payable at death (like an insurance beneficiary designation) is either:
- not sufficient to overcome a member’s express contractual right to change beneficiaries under the association’s bylaws, and/or
- insufficient to establish the kind of clear, binding “irrevocability” that would cut off the member’s power to amend.

At minimum, absent a written or otherwise clearly enforceable irrevocable-beneficiary agreement, the better view is that Lee’s oral promise did not eliminate his contractual right to change the beneficiary under the bylaws. Dana may have had a potential claim for breach against Lee’s estate (damages for inducing the marriage), but that does not automatically defeat the association’s contractual rules or override the later beneficiary designation made in accordance with the bylaws.

2) Effect of the beneficiary designations Lee actually made
Lee first filed the association’s standard form naming Dana as beneficiary after the wedding. Under bylaws that permit changes, that naming was still subject to being revoked.

Several years later, Lee followed the bylaws procedure: he filed a sworn request and named his two children from the earlier marriage. Because the bylaws allowed that exact method of changing beneficiaries, the later designation is effective unless Dana can show that Lee had forfeited that right by a binding irrevocable agreement—something she cannot establish here beyond an oral promise.

3) Who is entitled to the proceeds?
Because:
- the association’s bylaws expressly allowed beneficiary changes,
- Dana’s beneficiary status was therefore not inherently vested/irrevocable, and
- Lee exercised the bylaws-granted power by making a later, procedurally compliant change,

the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Dana’s strongest potential recourse would be a claim for breach of the oral promise against Lee’s estate (rather than a superior right to the proceeds), but on these facts she cannot defeat the later valid beneficiary change made under the association’s bylaws.

Conclusion: The children, as the later designated beneficiaries under the association’s valid bylaws procedure, have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

Dana’s claim is weaker. The proceeds will go to the children as the beneficiaries Lee validly designated when he followed the association’s by-laws.

1) Contract/third-party beneficiary theory (Dana)
Dana can frame Lee’s pre-marriage promise as an agreement to name her as beneficiary, making her a third-party beneficiary with enforceable rights.

But the problem facts cut against enforceability:

- The promise was not reduced to any written agreement signed by Lee. Because the promise was made “Before marrying Dana” and expressly in consideration of the marriage, many jurisdictions treat such marriage-consideration promises as within the statute of frauds requiring a writing (or otherwise as incapable of being enforced without one). If the statute of frauds applies, Dana cannot enforce the oral promise against the estate or otherwise compel payment of the policy proceeds.
- Even setting the statute-of-frauds question aside, a fraternal benefit association certificate and its beneficiary designations are controlled by the association’s by-laws. Here, the by-laws expressly permitted a member to change beneficiaries later by filing the required sworn request. That means beneficiary designations are, by rule, revocable unless the member has taken steps consistent with the by-laws to make the beneficiary designation irrevocable.

Lee did not sign any writing that would eliminate his by-law power to change beneficiaries. So Dana’s oral promise did not, by itself, create a right to proceeds superior to the later beneficiary change made in strict compliance with the by-laws.

2) Effect of the later beneficiary change (children)
After the marriage, Lee initially named Dana as beneficiary using the association’s standard form. However, the by-laws allowed later changes by sworn request. Several years later, Lee complied with that procedure and named his children from an earlier marriage as beneficiaries.

As between Dana and the later-designated children, the association will pay according to the most recent valid designation made under its rules. Since Lee retained and exercised the contractual/structural right to change beneficiaries (and did so properly), Dana did not acquire a vested entitlement to the proceeds that could defeat the later designation.

Conclusion
The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds because Lee validly changed the beneficiary designation under the association’s by-laws, and Dana’s reliance on an unenforceable or non-vested oral pre-marriage promise (without a signed writing eliminating revocation) cannot override the later properly filed beneficiary change.

Dana’s likely remedy, if any, would be a claim for damages for breach of promise—against Lee’s estate—not a right to recover the policy proceeds from the association superior to the later designated beneficiaries.

The fraternal insurance association’s bylaws control who is entitled to the certificate proceeds. In a fraternal benefit society, the contract is generally between the member and the society, and the society (and its bylaws) determine how beneficiaries may be designated and changed. A named beneficiary typically has no vested right in the proceeds until the member’s death; the member’s power to change the beneficiary—if preserved by the bylaws—defeats claims based only on prior designations or informal promises.

1) Effect of Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise
Dana’s best argument is that Lee’s promise (“if you marry me, I’ll make you beneficiary”) is supported by consideration (Dana’s marriage) and is therefore enforceable as a contract between Lee and Dana, potentially creating an equitable entitlement (e.g., a constructive trust) to prevent Lee from reneging.

However, even if the promise is enforceable as a contract between Lee and Dana, it does not automatically override the insurance association’s contractual governance of beneficiary changes. The association was not a party to the oral promise, and the bylaws expressly gave the member the ability to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. That means Dana’s “beneficiary status” was not made irrevocable by anything done in accordance with the certificate/bylaws.

2) Effect of the later sworn beneficiary change
After Dana’s initial designation as beneficiary, Lee later complied with the association’s bylaws by filing the required sworn request naming his children as the new beneficiaries. Because the bylaws authorized that change, Lee’s later designation is the operative one at his death.

Courts commonly hold that where the member reserved the right to change beneficiaries under the society’s bylaws, the proceeds go to the last beneficiary properly designated in accordance with those bylaws, even if the member previously promised another person that the earlier designation would be honored. If Dana had an enforceable contract claim based on the oral promise, her practical remedy is usually against Lee’s estate (or under an equitable theory directed at Lee’s successors), not a right to defeat the association’s obligation to pay the beneficiary last designated under its rules.

3) Remedy implication
Dana likely could argue for an equitable remedy (e.g., reimbursement or a constructive-trust-like claim) if she can prove the oral promise was a binding agreement and that the children (or Lee’s estate) should not be allowed to retain the full proceeds in equity. But on the question presented—who has the better claim to the insurance proceeds under the certificate/bylaws—the children prevail because Lee followed the bylaws to change the beneficiary, and Dana did not secure an irrevocable beneficiary right in a manner recognized by the association (and there was no written agreement “memorializing that promise”).

Conclusion
The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. The association’s bylaws authorized beneficiary changes by sworn request, and Lee properly exercised that reserved right shortly before his death. Dana’s oral pre-marriage promise and reliance may give her a separate contractual/equitable claim (often for damages or equitable relief), but it does not defeat the later beneficiary change that complied with the association’s governing rules.

Dana likely does not have the better claim to the insurance proceeds as against the fraternal association; the children’s later beneficiary designation should control.

1. Effect of the association’s by-laws and the certificate
Fraternal benefit associations typically treat beneficiary designations as governed by the member’s contract with the association (certificate + bylaws). Here, the bylaws expressly allow the member to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. Lee did so and named his two children from an earlier marriage. As a result, when Lee died, the “current” beneficiary under the association’s governing documents was the children’s designation, not Dana’s earlier designation.

2. Dana’s oral promise does not automatically override the reserved right to change
Dana’s claim rests on an oral promise made before marriage that she would be made beneficiary. Even if Lee’s promise induced Dana to marry him (which could supply consideration for an ordinary contract claim), the association and the certificate/bylaws still control who is entitled to the proceeds at death.

The key point is that, absent a legally effective restriction on Lee’s contractual right to change beneficiaries, Lee was permitted by the bylaws to revoke Dana as beneficiary. Dana’s earlier filing naming her does not “lock in” her status if the governing documents allow later changes.

3. Lack of a written agreement undercuts any argument that Lee surrendered the right to change
The prompt emphasizes that no written agreement memorialized Lee’s promise. While an oral promise can sometimes be enforceable as a contract, courts are reluctant to let a beneficiary use an informal promise to defeat the member’s explicit bylaw-based power to change beneficiaries—particularly in the fraternal insurance context where the association’s obligations run to the designated beneficiary under its rules.

Accordingly, Dana’s oral promise is better understood as potentially giving rise to a breach-of-promise claim (e.g., damages or, in some jurisdictions, equitable relief against Lee’s estate or the later beneficiaries if a court finds an enforceable contract not to change), rather than as conferring a superior right to the proceeds payable under the association’s latest beneficiary designation.

4. Who gets the proceeds now?
Because Lee complied with the bylaw procedure and named the children as the new beneficiaries years later, the association should pay the proceeds to the children as the last validly designated beneficiaries.

Conclusion: The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds because Lee’s later beneficiary change—authorized by the association’s bylaws—superseded Dana’s earlier designation. Dana’s oral pre-marriage promise, especially given the lack of a written restriction, is unlikely to defeat the children’s right to receive the proceeds under the certificate and bylaws (though Dana may have had, at most, a separate claim for breach against Lee’s estate or other parties depending on jurisdiction).

Dana’s claim rests on Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise to make her the beneficiary. The children’s claim rests on the association’s governing instruments—Lee’s later, properly filed change of beneficiary under the association’s by-laws. On these facts, the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

1. Under a fraternal insurance association’s by-laws, the named beneficiary generally does not acquire a vested right at the time of designation
A member’s beneficiary designation is typically treated as revocable unless the member has taken steps required by the association (or by statute) to make the beneficiary designation irrevocable. Where, as here, the by-laws explicitly allow the member to change beneficiaries later by sworn request, the member retains the power to revoke or replace the beneficiary. As a result, the “first” beneficiary designation (Dana’s naming after the wedding) is usually not final or controlling at the member’s death if the member later complies with the by-law procedure to designate someone else.

So, Dana is correct that she was named initially “after the wedding,” but that does not defeat the later beneficiary change that complied with the by-laws.

2. Lee’s oral promise does not, by itself, create an irrevocable beneficiary right against the association
Dana’s best argument is that the marriage itself supplied consideration and that the oral promise created an enforceable contract. However, even if Dana can establish a contract claim against Lee (or Lee’s estate) based on reliance/consideration, the critical issue for entitlement to the policy proceeds is whether that oral promise prevents Lee from exercising his by-law right to change beneficiaries.

Because no written agreement memorialized the promise and nothing in the facts suggests Lee complied with any requirement that an agreement making a beneficiary irrevocable must be in writing (or otherwise executed in the manner required to bind the association), Dana generally cannot use an oral promise to override the association’s by-laws and Lee’s later sworn request.

In other words: Dana may have a contract-based claim for damages or reimbursement, but she typically cannot displace the later beneficiary designation that the by-laws authorize.

3. The association paid out (or is payable) according to the valid, last designation filed under the by-laws
Lee followed the association’s standard procedure years later and named his two children from an earlier marriage as beneficiaries. Assuming the procedure was done properly (the prompt says he “followed that procedure”), that later designation is the one that controls payment. The association’s administrative rules effectively determine who has the superior claim to the proceeds at the time of death.

4. Who has the better claim?
Because:
- Lee had the express by-law power to change beneficiaries;
- the later change complied with that procedure; and
- Dana’s asserted entitlement is based on an oral promise that—without a written/required irrevocability instrument—ordinarily cannot override the by-law-governed revocability of beneficiary designations,

the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

Dana’s likely remedy, if any, would be a separate claim against Lee’s estate for breach of contract or for equitable relief (often at most in the form of damages), rather than a superior right to the proceeds against the properly designated later beneficiaries.

Conclusion: The children have the better claim to the proceeds under the association’s by-laws and Lee’s later valid beneficiary designation.

Dana’s claim is weaker. The proceeds ordinarily are determined by the beneficiary designation rules set by the fraternal insurance association and by-laws, not by an unrecorded promise made before marriage.

1) Contract framework: by-laws control and the member kept the power to change
When Lee became a member and obtained coverage, a contract relationship formed not only with the association but also incorporating the association’s by-laws. Those by-laws expressly allowed a member to change beneficiaries later by filing the required sworn request. That means the beneficiary named after the wedding (Dana) did not obtain an ownership right in the proceeds that was immune from later change; her interest was defeasible until Lee’s death.

So even though Lee initially filed the standard form naming Dana after the marriage, the by-laws gave Lee continuing authority to alter that designation.

2) The oral pre-marriage promise does not, by itself, override the by-laws
Dana argues that the pre-marriage oral promise (“if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary”) plus the fact that she married him gives her a contractual right to the proceeds. Marriage can be valid consideration for an agreement, so Dana may have a contract claim against Lee (or his estate) for breach.

But the key issue is whether that oral promise can deprive the association contract of Lee’s by-law-based power to change beneficiaries. Most courts treat the by-laws’ beneficiary-change mechanism as controlling as to who is legally entitled to the proceeds, absent some legally effective step taken by the member to make the designation irrevocable (for example, an assignment, surrender, or a written waiver/irrevocable designation recognized under the association’s rules). Dana has no such written irrevocability here—no agreement was ever signed, and nothing suggests Lee ever relinquished his by-law right to change.

Accordingly, Lee’s later compliance with the by-laws—filing the sworn request naming his children—generally controls who is entitled to the insurance proceeds.

3) Later beneficiary change likely defeats Dana’s entitlement; at most she may have a separate remedy
The children were named in the manner permitted by the by-laws, and they therefore are the rightful “beneficiaries” under the insurance contract. Dana’s remedy, if any, would more likely be limited to pursuing Lee’s estate for breach of the promise (damages), rather than taking the proceeds despite a valid subsequent beneficiary designation authorized by the contract’s by-laws.

4) Likely result
Because Lee retained and exercised the by-law power to change beneficiaries, and because Dana cannot point to a legally effective irrevocation of that power, the children have the better claim to the proceeds under the association’s beneficiary-change rules.

Bottom line: the children, as the later properly designated beneficiaries, have the stronger legal entitlement to the insurance proceeds. Dana’s strongest theory would be a contract/breach claim for damages against Lee’s estate, not an override of the subsequent beneficiary change.

Dana has the weaker claim to the life-insurance proceeds; the children have the better claim because the fraternal association’s governing contract (the certificate plus its by-laws) gives the member the continuing right to change beneficiaries, and Lee did so in the manner required.

1) The operative source of rights is the beneficiary designation under the certificate/by-laws
A fraternal benefit association’s obligation to pay is generally determined by the certificate and the by-laws that form part of the contract between (i) the member and (ii) the society. Those by-laws here expressly allow a member to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. Lee did exactly that years later and named his children.

As a result, when Lee died, the association would have been obligated (under its bylaws) to pay the proceeds to the beneficiary named in the last effective designation—i.e., the children—not to someone named earlier.

2) Dana’s pre-marriage oral promise does not, by itself, override a later properly filed beneficiary change
Dana’s argument is essentially: “Lee promised me orally that I would be the beneficiary, and I relied by marrying him.”

Even assuming Lee’s promise was sincere and would support some personal claim against Lee, an earlier oral promise typically does not make a beneficiary designation irrevocable—especially where the by-laws expressly permit later changes and no written restriction was created. In other words, Dana can argue breach of promise, but she usually cannot use an oral promise to defeat a later beneficiary designation that complies with the association’s procedures.

3) The lack of a signed/written agreement is a key problem for Dana’s attempt to obtain proceeds
Dana’s strongest “contract” theory would require an enforceable agreement that prevented Lee from changing the beneficiary (or at least created an enforceable equitable interest that could bind the proceeds). But there was no written agreement memorializing the promise that could be enforced as such.

Courts commonly treat agreements to provide for someone at death (including agreements made in consideration of marriage to name a beneficiary) as subject to statute-of-frauds-type requirements or, at minimum, requiring clear proof of an agreement intended to be legally binding and to limit the member’s rights. Here, Dana has only an oral promise and no document that restricted Lee’s by-law power to change beneficiaries. That weighs heavily against granting Dana priority over the later designation.

4) At most, Dana likely has an in personam claim (breach), not an in rem claim (to the policy proceeds)
Because Lee retained the contractual power to change beneficiaries and exercised it, Dana’s remedy is more likely personal rather than proprietary—e.g., a claim for breach of the promise against Lee’s estate (or, depending on the jurisdiction and proof, possibly other equitable relief against Lee’s estate).

But once the association properly changed the designation under its bylaws and paid (or is owed to pay) the proceeds to the later beneficiary, Dana generally cannot “reach through” to take the proceeds from the person named in the final designation.

Conclusion
The children have the better claim. Lee’s later, by-law-compliant beneficiary change controls the insurance proceeds. Dana’s pre-marriage oral promise may support a breach-of-promise claim against Lee (or his estate), but it does not overcome the association’s contractual scheme permitting beneficiary changes and Lee’s subsequent designation of the children.

Dana has the weaker claim, and the children have the better claim to the proceeds.

1. Baseline rule: the designated beneficiary has only an expectancy unless the member bound himself to make the designation irrevocable  
In a fraternal benefit/assessment insurance arrangement, the member’s promise and the certificate/by-laws form the controlling “contract” governing beneficiary rights. If the by-laws reserve to the member the right to change beneficiaries, the person named initially generally does not acquire a vested right in the policy proceeds; the named beneficiary is typically only an expectancy that can be cut off by a later effective change filed in the manner the by-laws require.

Here, Lee’s association by-laws expressly allowed later beneficiary changes by filing a sworn request. That reservation of the right to change strongly indicates that Dana’s post-marriage designation was not intended to be irrevocable under the insurance contract.

2. Dana’s reliance on Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise does not displace the later by-law-compliant change  
Dana’s argument is essentially: “I gave consideration (marriage) in reliance on Lee’s promise to make me beneficiary, so I should prevail.”

Even assuming the marriage could supply consideration for an ordinary promise-based contract claim against Lee, Dana still faces a major problem: she must show that Lee contracted to surrender his by-law right to change the beneficiary (i.e., that Dana’s beneficiary status became irrevocable). On these facts, there is no written agreement memorializing such an undertaking, and Lee did exactly what the by-laws permit—he later filed the required sworn request naming new beneficiaries.

Courts generally do not allow an earlier oral promise to override the association’s by-laws and the insured’s retained right to change beneficiaries, particularly where (a) the insured’s ability to change beneficiaries is expressly reserved and (b) there is no written, signed instrument showing an intent to make the original beneficiary designation irrevocable.

3. The children’s claim tracks the controlling contract terms and procedure  
Lee complied with the by-laws later and named his two children as beneficiaries by the required sworn request. At Lee’s death, the legally effective designation is the one on file after that change. Under the insurance contract/by-laws framework, the association should pay the named beneficiaries at death—here, the children.

4. What Dana might still have (but not what she’s asking for)  
Dana’s stronger potential relief would be a claim for breach of Lee’s promise (or possibly equitable relief like a constructive trust) against Lee’s estate—depending on the jurisdiction’s willingness to enforce an oral promise despite the by-laws. But that is different from entitlement to the insurance proceeds as against the properly redesignated beneficiaries under the association’s controlling rules.

Conclusion  
Because the association’s by-laws reserved and permitted later beneficiary changes, and Lee made an effective later change naming his children, the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Dana’s claim based on Lee’s earlier oral promise (without a written irrevocability agreement) is unlikely to defeat the later by-law-compliant beneficiary designation.

# Analysis of Beneficiary Claim to Insurance Proceeds

## The Children's Superior Claim

The children have the substantially better claim to the insurance proceeds. Although Dana has a sympathetic position based on Lee's oral promise, multiple legal principles favor the children's claim.

## Lee's Authority to Change Beneficiaries

The fundamental issue is whether Lee retained the contractual right to change beneficiaries after initially designating Dana. The association's by-laws explicitly permitted members to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee properly exercised this contractual right. This is not a case where Lee lacked authority to make the change—the association's governing documents expressly authorized it.

When a policyholder or certificate holder has reserved the right to change beneficiaries (as Lee did through the by-laws), the later change of beneficiary designation generally revokes the earlier one. This is a matter of contract interpretation and the policyholder's contractual rights under the insurance instrument itself. Lee's later designation of his children superseded his earlier designation of Dana as a matter of contract law.

## The Oral Promise Problem

Dana's claim rests entirely on an oral promise Lee made before the marriage. This promise faces several substantial obstacles:

**Statute of Frauds**: Many jurisdictions apply the statute of frauds to contracts involving life insurance beneficiary designations, particularly when the promise is made by someone other than the insurance company itself. Dana's claim is based on Lee's personal promise, not on a contract with the association. The promise was not reduced to writing and was not signed by Lee, the party to be charged. This oral promise likely cannot be enforced under the statute of frauds.

**Lack of Consideration**: While Dana's marriage to Lee could arguably constitute consideration for his promise, courts often find this problematic. The promise was made as an inducement to marriage, but Dana would have had to marry Lee for the promise to be enforceable—creating a circular dependency. Moreover, Dana's performance of the marriage obligation may not be viewed as consideration flowing to Lee in a way that supports an enforceable contract, since both parties presumably benefited from the marriage itself.

**Parol Evidence Limitations**: Even if the statute of frauds did not apply, Dana would face parol evidence issues. Once Lee formally designated Dana as beneficiary through the association's written form, that written designation became the operative contract between Lee and the association. Dana's oral promise cannot be used to modify or enforce rights against the association beyond what the written designation provides. The oral promise might support a personal claim against Lee's estate, but it cannot override the contractual rights created by the written beneficiary designation forms filed with the association.

## The Nature of Beneficiary Rights

A critical distinction exists between Dana's potential contract claim against Lee (or his estate) and her claim to insurance proceeds. The insurance proceeds are not Dana's property; they belong to the association until properly distributed according to the beneficiary designation on file. Dana's oral promise from Lee does not give her any contractual rights against the association itself. The association's obligation is to pay according to the valid beneficiary designation currently in effect—which names the children.

If Dana had a valid claim, it would be against Lee's estate for breach of a personal promise, not against the insurance proceeds. But given the statute of frauds and consideration issues, even that claim would likely fail.

## The Later Designation's Effect

Lee's filing of the sworn request to change beneficiaries to his children was a valid exercise of his contractual rights under the association's by-laws. This later designation is:

1. **In writing** (the sworn request form)

# Analysis of Claim to Insurance Proceeds

## The Children's Superior Claim

The children have the stronger claim to the insurance proceeds. This analysis requires examining the enforceability of Dana's claim against the subsequent valid designation.

## Dana's Oral Promise Claim

Dana's claim based on Lee's pre-marriage oral promise faces substantial obstacles:

**Contract Formation Issues**: An oral promise to name someone as a beneficiary on a life insurance policy is generally not enforceable as a binding contract. Most jurisdictions require such promises to be in writing, either under general contract law principles or specific insurance regulations. The Statute of Frauds may apply, as the promise concerns a matter that cannot be performed within one year (it depends on Lee's lifespan). Without a written agreement signed by Lee, Dana cannot enforce the pre-marriage promise as an independent contractual obligation.

**No Equitable Estoppel**: While some courts have recognized equitable estoppel in life insurance beneficiary disputes, the facts here do not strongly support it. Estoppel typically requires: (1) a clear and unequivocal promise, (2) reliance by the promisee, and (3) unconscionable injury if the promise is not enforced. Although Dana married Lee in reliance on his promise, she did not change her legal position in a way that would make enforcement equitable. She would have married Lee regardless of insurance benefits, and the promise was merely incidental to the marriage decision. Courts are reluctant to impose estoppel obligations on insurance carriers based on oral promises between individuals.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Initial Beneficiary Designation

Dana's stronger argument rests on Lee's actual designation of her as beneficiary after the marriage. However, this designation was subsequently revoked.

**The Revocation Problem**: The critical issue is whether Lee's later change of beneficiary legally extinguished Dana's rights under the earlier designation. The answer is unambiguously yes. Life insurance beneficiary designations are revocable by nature unless the policy or contract explicitly provides otherwise. Nothing in the facts suggests that Lee's initial designation was irrevocable. The association's by-laws explicitly permitted members to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee properly followed that procedure.

**Legal Principle**: A revocable beneficiary designation creates no vested property right in the beneficiary. The beneficiary's interest is contingent and subject to the policyholder's absolute right to change it at any time before death. When Lee filed the sworn request naming his children as beneficiaries, he exercised his contractual right to revoke Dana's designation. Once revoked, Dana's interest terminated entirely.

## The Children's Position

The children, as the final designated beneficiaries, hold an unencumbered claim to the proceeds. They are named in the most recent valid beneficiary designation on file with the association. The association's by-laws permitted the change, Lee followed the required procedure, and no legal impediment prevented him from making the change.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim. Dana cannot enforce the pre-marriage oral promise due to lack of written documentation and the general principle that such promises are not binding. More significantly, even though Lee did initially designate Dana after the marriage, he subsequently exercised his contractual right to revoke that designation and name his children instead. Life insurance beneficiary designations are revocable unless expressly made irrevocable, and Lee's revocation was valid and effective. The proceeds should go to the children as the designated beneficiaries under the final, valid designation.

Dana’s claim rests on Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise to make her the beneficiary. The children’s claim rests on the association’s governing instruments—Lee’s later, properly filed change of beneficiary under the association’s by-laws. On these facts, the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

1. Under a fraternal insurance association’s by-laws, the named beneficiary generally does not acquire a vested right at the time of designation
A member’s beneficiary designation is typically treated as revocable unless the member has taken steps required by the association (or by statute) to make the beneficiary designation irrevocable. Where, as here, the by-laws explicitly allow the member to change beneficiaries later by sworn request, the member retains the power to revoke or replace the beneficiary. As a result, the “first” beneficiary designation (Dana’s naming after the wedding) is usually not final or controlling at the member’s death if the member later complies with the by-law procedure to designate someone else.

So, Dana is correct that she was named initially “after the wedding,” but that does not defeat the later beneficiary change that complied with the by-laws.

2. Lee’s oral promise does not, by itself, create an irrevocable beneficiary right against the association
Dana’s best argument is that the marriage itself supplied consideration and that the oral promise created an enforceable contract. However, even if Dana can establish a contract claim against Lee (or Lee’s estate) based on reliance/consideration, the critical issue for entitlement to the policy proceeds is whether that oral promise prevents Lee from exercising his by-law right to change beneficiaries.

Because no written agreement memorialized the promise and nothing in the facts suggests Lee complied with any requirement that an agreement making a beneficiary irrevocable must be in writing (or otherwise executed in the manner required to bind the association), Dana generally cannot use an oral promise to override the association’s by-laws and Lee’s later sworn request.

In other words: Dana may have a contract-based claim for damages or reimbursement, but she typically cannot displace the later beneficiary designation that the by-laws authorize.

3. The association paid out (or is payable) according to the valid, last designation filed under the by-laws
Lee followed the association’s standard procedure years later and named his two children from an earlier marriage as beneficiaries. Assuming the procedure was done properly (the prompt says he “followed that procedure”), that later designation is the one that controls payment. The association’s administrative rules effectively determine who has the superior claim to the proceeds at the time of death.

4. Who has the better claim?
Because:
- Lee had the express by-law power to change beneficiaries;
- the later change complied with that procedure; and
- Dana’s asserted entitlement is based on an oral promise that—without a written/required irrevocability instrument—ordinarily cannot override the by-law-governed revocability of beneficiary designations,

the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

Dana’s likely remedy, if any, would be a separate claim against Lee’s estate for breach of contract or for equitable relief (often at most in the form of damages), rather than a superior right to the proceeds against the properly designated later beneficiaries.

Conclusion: The children have the better claim to the proceeds under the association’s by-laws and Lee’s later valid beneficiary designation.

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. 

The threshold issue is whether the oral promise Lee made to Dana is enforceable. Under the Statute of Frauds, a contract made in consideration of marriage must be in writing to be enforceable. Because Lee’s promise to name Dana as the beneficiary was made orally in exchange for her agreement to marry him, the agreement falls squarely within the Statute of Frauds. Although Dana performed her part of the bargain by marrying Lee, the doctrine of part performance generally does not apply to contracts made in consideration of marriage; the act of marriage itself is insufficient to remove the oral agreement from the requirements of the Statute of Frauds. Consequently, the oral promise is unenforceable.

Because the promise is unenforceable, Dana’s initial designation as the beneficiary was not supported by a binding contract that would have rendered her interest irrevocable. In the context of fraternal insurance associations and standard life insurance policies, a member typically retains the right to change beneficiaries at any time, provided they follow the association’s established procedures. 

The association’s by-laws explicitly permitted Lee to change his beneficiary by filing a sworn request. When Lee complied with this procedure several years later to name his children as the new beneficiaries, he exercised a valid right under the insurance contract. Since Dana lacked a written, enforceable contract that would have vested her with an irrevocable interest in the proceeds, Lee remained free to change the beneficiary designation at his discretion. Accordingly, the children, as the final designated beneficiaries under the validly executed change of beneficiary, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.
